narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, LSAT score of 28, Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, and full-time student status—and calculated a 96% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate successfully. The decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 academic decile, which ranked you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average—and this low performance strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, again placing you in the lower ranks compared to peers averaging around the 6th decile, further reinforcing the model's concern about your early academic trajectory. Your LSAT score of 28, well below the dataset average of about 37, also contributed significantly by signaling lower-than-typical readiness for law school rigor, nudging the prediction even more toward failure. While your full-time status aligns with most students and your middle-income bracket is common, these didn't outweigh the red flags from your grades and test scores. In comparison to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger high school deciles, higher GPAs around 3.2, and LSATs near 37, your metrics stood out as notably weaker predictors of bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your profile diverges from successful patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program, or targeting schools with more holistic admissions that value your full-time commitment. Building a clearer track record of academic improvement could shift future predictions in your favor. With targeted effort, you can reshape how models—and admissions teams—view your bar exam prospects.
